ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists for beef cooked in yellow pepper sauce specifically; closest_parent 'beef dish' (FOODON_03302051) used as the nearest defensible anchor.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0CKP (Beef, cooked) is the closest parent; no specific code exists for beef in yellow pepper sauce in the FoodEx2 catalogue.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: SNOMED CT 226529007 'Beef dish' is the closest available parent concept; no specific code for yellow pepper sauce beef exists.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical restaurant serving of Fresh Tender Beef Cooked in Yellow Pepper Sauce is approximately 300 g, representing a shared main dish for one to two persons.

nutrient_source.title: Beef, short loin, all grades, cooked, broiled — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy; adapted with sauce composition estimate
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service — FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No direct entry for beef in yellow pepper sauce exists in HPB Singapore Foodo, USDA FoodData Central, or FAO/INFOODS SEA databases. Nutrient values are estimated by adapting USDA SR Legacy data for cooked fatty beef (approximately 70% of dish weight) combined with a yellow pepper sauce component (approximately 30% of dish weight, contributing additional fat, sodium, and minor carbohydrate from starch and sugar). The fatty beef cut (e.g. beef short rib / ribeye slices) is characteristic of this dish style. Values represent a best-estimate composite. Confidence is medium.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for beef in yellow pepper sauce. GI is estimated at 28 based on the analogous category of mixed meat-and-sauce dishes with low starch content. The dominant macronutrient is protein and fat; carbohydrate content is low (approximately 4.2 g per 100 g), primarily from starch thickener and minor sugar. Low GI is consistent with high-protein, high-fat, low-carbohydrate cooked meat dishes as documented in Atkinson et al. (2008) and the University of Sydney GI Database. Glycemic load per 300 g serving = (28 × 12.6 g available carbohydrate) / 100 ≈ 3.5, classified as low GL.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content from yellow pepper sauce, soy sauce, and seasoning
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size to 150 g or less per serving, as the estimated sodium content of approximately 420 mg per 100 g (approximately 1260 mg per 300 g serving) represents over 50% of the recommended daily sodium limit of 2000 mg; requesting reduced-sauce or sauce-on-side preparation is advisable.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low carbohydrate content and low glycemic index
type2_diabetes.guidance: With an estimated glycemic index of 28 and approximately 4.2 g carbohydrate per 100 g, this dish has a low glycemic impact and is generally suitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes in moderate portions; however, the high fat content from fatty beef cuts should be considered in the context of overall caloric and lipid management.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat and cholesterol from fatty beef cuts combined with high sodium from sauce
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The combination of approximately 5.2 g saturated fat per 100 g, an estimated 58 mg cholesterol per 100 g, and high sodium from the sauce presents a moderate cardiovascular risk; individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit consumption to small portions and choose leaner beef preparations where possible.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Fresh Tender Beef Cooked in Yellow Pepper Sauce (黄椒酱煮鲜肥牛, Huáng jiāo jiàng zhǔ xiān féi niú) is a Chinese-style braised beef dish featuring thinly sliced fatty beef — typically ribeye or short rib cuts — simmered in a fragrant yellow pepper sauce made from yellow chillies, garlic, ginger, soy sauce, and starch-thickened seasoning. The dish is characterised by its vibrant yellow-orange colour, a mildly spicy and aromatic flavour profile described as 椒香辣爽 (pepper-fragrant and refreshingly spicy), and a tender, melt-in-the-mouth texture from the fatty beef slices. It is a popular restaurant dish in Singapore's Chinese dining scene, often served as a shared main course with steamed rice. Nutritionally, the dish is high in protein (approximately 14.5 g per 100 g) and fat (approximately 13.5 g per 100 g), with low carbohydrate content (approximately 4.2 g per 100 g), giving it a low glycemic index of approximately 28. Sodium content is elevated at approximately 420 mg per 100 g due to the sauce components, making portion control important for individuals managing hypertension or cardiovascular disease. The dish is dairy-free and suitable for halal diets only if prepared with halal-certified beef and sauces. It is not suitable for vegetarians or vegans.

confidence_reason: No direct entry for this specific dish was found in HPB Singapore Foodo, USDA FoodData Central, FAO/INFOODS SEA, or ICMR-NIN databases. Nutrient values are estimated by adapting USDA SR Legacy cooked fatty beef data combined with a sauce composition estimate. GI is analogously estimated from the University of Sydney GI Database category for low-carbohydrate cooked meat dishes. Confidence is medium due to the composite estimation approach.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are estimated composites; no exact database entry exists for this dish. Primary adaptation basis is USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy cooked fatty beef data. 2. vitamin_a_mcg_rae is -1 as it is not reliably reported for this dish type in the adapted source data. 3. folate_mcg_dfe is -1 as it is not reported in the adapted source. 4. vitamin_d_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the adapted source. 5. vitamin_e_mg is -1 as it is not reported in the adapted source. 6. selenium_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the adapted source. 7. GI is an analogous estimate; no direct GI measurement for this dish exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. 8. The input food_group 'Oils, seasonings and condiments' has been corrected to 'Meat and alternatives' as the dish is a beef-dominant main course. 9. Halal status is marked false as it cannot be confirmed without restaurant-level certification; the dish may be halal if prepared with certified ingredients. 10. Price data (SGD 46.80) from field survey data is preserved as contextual metadata but is not used as a nutritional source.
